content:
The Hidden Data Trail: Ethics, Trust, and Anonymous Surveys

At first glance, an end of course survey may seem harmless. A course ends, participants answer a few questions, and management uses the results to improve future learning. That sounds responsible. It sounds practical. It sounds like data science doing exactly what it should do: collecting feedback, finding patterns, and helping people make better decisions.

But ethics in data science often begins with a simple question: To what extent can anyone be harmed or negatively impacted by this process?*

In this case, participants in a course are told that their survey responses will remain anonymous. Yet the survey requires them to log in with their organizational username and password, use their web browser, and identify the course they completed. Even though the survey does not directly ask for their names, the design creates a hidden data trail. That trail may include login records, IP addresses, browser data, timestamps, course information, and system logs. When these details are combined, the survey may not be truly anonymous at all.

This matters because trust is the foundation of ethical data work. If students are told their answers are anonymous, they should be able to believe that. If the organization cannot guarantee anonymity, it should say so clearly. Otherwise, the survey risks becoming misleading, unfair, and harmful. The following are six ethical issues that every student of data science or AI ethics should understand.

1. Anonymous Does Not Mean “We Did Not Ask for Your Name”

One of the biggest ethical problems in this scenario is the claim of anonymity. Many people assume that data is anonymous if a person’s name is not written on the form. That is not enough.

A survey is truly anonymous only when responses cannot reasonably be connected back to a subject person. In this case, students must log in with their username and password. That means the system may know who accessed the survey, when they accessed it, and possibly what device or browser they used. Even if the analyst never sees the student’s name directly, the organization may still have enough metadata to identify them.

This is where the difference between anonymous and confidential becomes important. Anonymous means the identity is not collected or cannot be linked to the response. Confidential means the identity may be known or traceable, but access to that information is restricted. If login is required, the organization should not promise anonymity unless the system has been carefully designed to separate identity from responses.

2. Browser and Device Data Can Identify People

The second issue is the hidden information that can be collected when someone uses a web browser. Most people think they are only submitting the answers they type or click. But digital systems often collect more than users realize.

A browser can reveal technical details such as IP address, device type, operating system, browser version, screen size, language settings, plugins, fonts, and access time. These pieces of information may seem harmless by themselves. But together, they can create a digital fingerprint.

This is called browser fingerprinting. It means that a person may be recognized or tracked based on the unique combination of technical details from their device and browser. Even if names are removed, this metadata can sometimes be used to connect responses back to individuals.

This creates a serious privacy issue. The participants may believe they are giving feedback freely, while the system quietly collects enough information to identify or track them. Ethical data science requires more than good intentions. It requires technical awareness. Data analysts must ask not only, “What answers are in the survey?” but also, “What hidden data is being collected in the background?”

3. Course Information Can Become Identifying Information

The survey asks participants to name the course they completed. That seems reasonable because management wants to know which courses need redesign or updating. However, course information can become identifying when combined with other details.

For example, imagine that only five students completed a specialized course. If one person gives very negative feedback, it may not be difficult to identify who it is, especially if management knows the participants, the instructor, or the department. If the course is linked to a small group, a unique job role, or a specific time, the risk of identification increases.

This is known as a re-identification risk. Re-identification happens when supposedly anonymous data can be connected back to a person by combining it with other available information.

For this reason, ethical reporting should use aggregation rules. For example, management should only receive results for groups large enough to protect individual students. If fewer than a certain number of participants respond, the results should be combined with a larger group or withheld. This protects them from being singled out while still allowing the organization to learn from the data.

4. The First Survey Question May Shape the Answers

The first question asks participants whether the course met all its objectives, using a scale from strongly disagree to strongly agree. On the surface, this looks like a normal survey question. But its placement matters.

When a survey begins with a broad evaluation question, it can shape how participants think about the rest of the survey. This is called priming. If they begin by making an overall judgment, they may answer later questions in a way that supports that first impression. For example, if participants in the survey give a low score at the beginning, they may continue to think negatively or feel compelled to continue giving low scores. If they give a high score, they may hesitate to criticize specific parts later. If they believe instructors, managers, or executives might see their answers, they may avoid honest criticism.

5. Power Dynamics Can Silence Honest Feedback

Surveys do not happen in a vacuum. They happen inside relationships of power. In a college or workplace learning environment, students may depend on instructors, managers, departments, or organizations for grades, opportunities, employment, recommendations, or future training.

That power dynamic matters in this scenario. Even if no one intends to punish the participants for negative feedback, they may still fear consequences. If they are told the survey is anonymous but must log in to complete it, they may wonder: “Can they really see what I wrote?” That fear can reduce honesty.

This creates both an ethical problem and a data quality problem. Ethically, students deserve to give feedback without fear. In data science, management needs honest responses to make good decisions. If participants soften their criticism, exaggerate satisfaction, or avoid responding altogether, the data becomes less reliable.

6. Data Collected for Improvement Could Be Misused Later

The stated purpose of the survey is course improvement. Executive management wants to know which courses need redesign or updating. That is a legitimate purpose. However, another ethical concern is secondary use.

Secondary use occurs when data collected for one purpose is later used for another purpose. For example, survey responses collected for course redesign might later be used to judge instructors, evaluate departments, identify “difficult” students, or support performance decisions. Even if that is not the original plan, it can happen if clear boundaries are not established.

This is sometimes called mission creep or use creep. A project begins with a reasonable goal, but the data becomes tempting for other uses. To prevent this, the organization should create a written policy that limits how the data may be used. Students should be told who will see the data, how it will be reported, how long it will be kept, and whether it will ever be used for decisions about students, instructors, or employees. Ethical data collection requires informed consent. People should understand what they are agreeing to before they participate.

Conclusion: Trust Is Part of the Data

This survey scenario shows that privacy involves more than removing names. As data scientists, we must be aware that, login records, course details, browser information, timestamps, and other metadata can all be used to identify a person. When combined, these details can undermine a promise of anonymity.

Ethical data science requires honesty, transparency, privacy protection, fairness, informed consent, and accountability. It also requires courage. A good analyst must be willing to ask uncomfortable questions before turning data into decisions and inform management when there are ethical concerns. The central issue is not whether the surveys are useful but whether people can trust that the process will not adversely affect them.

Some organisations do not have ethical processes built-in to their administrative structure. The data scientist in this scenario will need document concerns to executive management before proceeding; inform them about the data limitations; be proactive in protecting participants by only releasing the results and not the raw data; and remain within professional and ethical boundaries.
title: The Hidden Data Trail

content:
Five Key Lessons in Communication for Data Analytics

In data analytics, communication is not a soft extra. It is central to the work. A data analyst may collect the data, clean it, analyze it, visualize it, and discover important patterns. But if those insights are not communicated clearly and meaningfully, much of the value can be lost. Data does not speak for itself. People interpret it, question it, and use it to make decisions. This means the role of the data analyst is not only to understand the numbers, but to help others understand what the numbers mean. At this stage in my own learning and reflection, I am becoming more aware that professional communication requires more than speaking well or writing clearly. It requires awareness of the person receiving the message. It requires sensitivity to context, culture, tone, format, and even body language. This is especially important in cross-cultural environments, where words, idioms, gestures, humour, silence, and tone may be understood differently. What feels clear to one person may feel abrupt to another. What sounds normal in one setting may be confusing, dismissive, or even disrespectful in another. For a data analyst, communication is about more than reporting findings. It is about building understanding, trust, and wise decision-making. Here are five key lessons that matter deeply in data analytics.

1. Know Your Audience

The first lesson of strong communication is knowing your audience. A data analyst may communicate with colleagues, clients, managers, executives, or non-technical stakeholders. Each audience requires a different approach. The way I explain a technical issue to another analyst may not be the same way I present findings to a manager. A colleague may want to understand the method. A client may want to know what the numbers mean. An executive may want the key insight, the risk, and the recommendation. Before communicating, we should pause and ask: Who is receiving this information? What do they already understand? What do they need to know? What decision are they trying to make? When we know our audience, we become better at choosing the right words, the right level of detail, and the right format. We avoid overwhelming people with unnecessary information, and we also avoid oversimplifying what needs careful explanation. Good communication begins with awareness.

2. Practice Good Email Etiquette

Email remains one of the most common tools of professional communication. Because of that, we should never treat it carelessly. A professional email should be clear, respectful, and purposeful. It should have a meaningful subject line, a respectful greeting, a direct message, a professional tone, and a clear next step. In data analytics, this matters because emails often accompany reports, dashboards, updates, questions, and recommendations. A poorly written email can make good analysis appear careless. A thoughtful email can help the recipient understand the purpose and value of the work. Good email etiquette also requires attention to tone. Since the person reading the message cannot hear our voice or see our face, the words carry more weight. A sentence that sounds harmless in our mind may feel cold or abrupt to someone else. That is why it helps to pause before sending. Read the message again. Ask whether it is clear, respectful, and complete. In professional communication, small details can shape how seriously our work is received.

3. Keep Executive Communication Direct and Concise

When communicating with managers or executives, being direct is very important. Leaders often have limited time and many decisions to make. They may not need every technical detail at the beginning. They need the main point, the business impact, the risk, and the recommendation. This does not mean details are unimportant. Details matter greatly in data analytics. But good communication knows how to layer information. The main insight should come first. The supporting evidence should follow. Additional details can be included for those who need to go deeper. For executives, communication should answer a practical question: What do I need to know, and what should I do next? A strong data analyst does not hide important findings under too much explanation. They respect the decision-maker’s time while still preserving accuracy and integrity. Clear executive communication is not about saying less because the work is simple. It is about saying what matters most, in the order that helps people act wisely.

4. Be Ready to Listen, Not Just Speak

Communication is not only about what we say. It is also about how well we listen. This is especially important in data analytics because our work often depends on understanding the real problem behind the request. A client may ask for a report, but what they really need is clarity about a decision. A manager may ask for numbers, but what they really need is insight into a trend. A colleague may challenge our conclusion, not to be difficult, but because they see something we may have missed. Good listening helps us ask better questions, avoid wrong assumptions, recognize concerns, and understand what people are truly trying to solve. If we are only focused on presenting our own findings, we may miss valuable information from the people we are serving. A good data analyst does not simply deliver information. A good data analyst listens for meaning. Listening helps transform data work from a technical exercise into a human conversation about needs, decisions, risks, and possibilities.

5. Be Conscious of Non-Verbal Communication

Communication is also shaped by what is not spoken. In face-to-face settings, our posture, facial expression, eye contact, gestures, and tone of voice all affect how our message is received. We may say the right words, but if our body language suggests impatience, distraction, or defensiveness, the message can lose its impact. This matters in meetings, presentations, interviews, client conversations, and team discussions. When presenting data, we should not only think about the charts and numbers. We should also think about how we are showing up as professionals. Are we calm? Are we attentive? Are we respectful when questioned? Are we open to feedback? Are we helping people feel included in the conversation? Even in writing, there is a kind of non-verbal communication. Formatting, punctuation, spacing, timing, and tone all send signals. A rushed message, an unclear subject line, or a poorly organized report can communicate carelessness, even when the content itself is useful. The message is not only in the words, but is also in the way the words are presented.

Final Reflection

Data analysts do more than just work with data. We help people understand what the data means. That requires technical skill, but it also requires emotional intelligence, cultural awareness, listening, clarity, and respect. We must know our audience and write with professionalism. We must communicate concisely with decision-makers. We must listen carefully. And we must be aware of the non-verbal signals that shape how our message is received. In the end, communication is what turns analysis into understanding. And understanding is what makes data useful.
title: Five Key Lessons in Communication

content:
Mathjax

Math equations can be rendered using Mathjax syntax with AMS symbol support.

Optionally enable this on a per-page basis by adding mathjax: true to your frontmatter.

Then, use $$ ... $$ on a line by itself to render a block equation:

$$ | Pr_{x \leftarrow P_{1}} [A(x) = 1] - Pr_{x \leftarrow P_{2}} [A(x) = 1] | < \text{negligible} $$

The raw version is:

$$ | Pr_{x \leftarrow P_{1}} [A(x) = 1] - Pr_{x \leftarrow P_{2}} [A(x) = 1] | < \text{negligible} $$

Write in-line equations with \\( ... \\) , like \( x^n / y \) . It’s easy!

Write in-line equations with `\\( ... \\)` , like \\( x^n / y \\) . It's easy!
title: Render Math With Mathjax

Blockquotes

The blockquote element represents content that is quoted from another source, optionally with a citation which must be within a footer or cite element, and optionally with in-line changes such as annotations and abbreviations.

Blockquote without attribution

Tiam, ad mint andaepu dandae nostion secatur sequo quae. Note that you can use Markdown syntax within a blockquote.

Blockquote with attribution

Don’t communicate by sharing memory, share memory by communicating.

— Rob Pike1

Tables

Tables aren’t part of the core Markdown spec, but Hugo supports supports them out-of-the-box.

Name Age
Bob 27
Alice 23

Inline Markdown within tables

Inline    Markdown    In    Table
italics bold strikethrough    code

Code Blocks

Code block with backticks

<!DOCTYPE html>
<html lang="en">
  <head>
    <meta charset="UTF-8" />
    <title>Example HTML5 Document</title>
  </head>
  <body>
    <p>Test</p>
  </body>
</html>

Code block indented with four spaces

<!DOCTYPE html>
<html lang="en">
<head>
  <meta charset="UTF-8">
  <title>Example HTML5 Document</title>
</head>
<body>
  <p>Test</p>
</body>
</html>

Code block with Hugo’s internal highlight shortcode

<!DOCTYPE html>
<html lang="en">
<head>
  <meta charset="UTF-8">
  <title>Example HTML5 Document</title>
</head>
<body>
  <p>Test</p>
</body>
</html>

List Types

Ordered List

  1. First item
  2. Second item
  3. Third item

Unordered List

  • List item
  • Another item
  • And another item

Nested list

  • Item
    1. First Sub-item
    2. Second Sub-item

Headings

The following HTML <h1>—<h6> elements represent six levels of section headings. <h1> is the highest section level while <h6> is the lowest.

H1

H2

H3

H4

H5
H6

Other Elements — abbr, sub, sup, kbd, mark

GIF is a bitmap image format.

H2O

Xn + Yn = Zn

Press CTRL+ALT+Delete to end the session.

Most salamanders are nocturnal, and hunt for insects, worms, and other small creatures.

  1. The above quote is excerpted from Rob Pike’s talk during Gopherfest, November 18, 2015. ↩︎
title: Markdown Syntax

content:
Emoji can be enabled in a Hugo project in a number of ways. :zap:

Emoji Support

The emojify function can be called directly in templates or Inline Shortcodes.

To enable emoji globally, set enableEmoji to true in your site’s configuration and then you can type emoji shorthand codes directly in content files; e.g.

The Emoji cheat sheet is a useful reference for emoji shorthand codes.

N.B. The above steps enable Unicode Standard emoji characters and sequences in Hugo, however the rendering of these glyphs depends on the browser and the platform. To style the emoji you can either use a third party emoji font or a font stack; e.g.

.emoji {
  font-family: Apple Color Emoji, Segoe UI Emoji, NotoColorEmoji, Segoe UI Symbol, Android Emoji, EmojiSymbols;
}
title: Emoji Support

content:
Hugo ships with several Embedded Shortcodes for rich content, along with a Privacy Config and a set of Simple Shortcodes that enable static and no-JS versions of various social media embeds.

X Simple Shortcode

{{< x user="GoHugoIO" id="1315233626070503424" >}}

Profile is a customizable single-page portfolio theme for Hugo featuring a contact form and blog. Made by @gurusabarishh.https://t.co/KwYcwfdy16

— GoHugo.io (@GoHugoIO) October 11, 2020

Vimeo Simple Shortcode

{{< vimeo 146022717 >}}


Youtube Simple Shortcode

{{< youtube w7Ft2ymGmfc >}}


Theme Custom Shortcodes

These shortcodes are not Hugo built-ins, but are provided by the theme.

Responsive Images with Cloudinary

You can learn more about this here.

Set the cloudinary_cloud_name parameter in your site config to use this shortcode.

{{< dynamic-img src="/my/image/on/cloudinary" title="A title for the image" >}}

Note that you do not include the file extension (e.g. .png) in the src parameter, as the shortcode will automatically determine the best quality and format for the user’s device.

Optionally, you can customize the general CSS styles for the image:

{{< dynamic-img src="/my/image/on/cloudinary" title="A title for the image" style="max-width:60%" >}}
title: Rich Content
